sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=56, col=16).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 68, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 67 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on the left blue square didn't move it. The blue squares are still at:
- Left: rows 54-58, cols 14-18
- Right: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
Let me think about this more carefully. I've been exploring the game mechanics, but I haven't figured out the goal yet. Let me review what I know:
1. The face has two blue squares at the bottom (rows 54-58).
2. The blue squares can move up (left moves 3 rows per action, right moves 2 rows per action).
3. The blue squares can move down (left moves 3 rows per action, right moves 2 rows per action).
4. The blue squares can move horizontally when I press MOUSE on certain regions (like the left eye socket).
5. The border has B's and W's, which seem to count actions.
6. Pressing SPACE doesn't complete the level.
7. Clicking on the blue squares doesn't move them.
8. Clicking on the center of the face doesn't move the blue squares.
9. Clicking on the right eye socket doesn't move the blue squares.
10. Clicking on the left eye socket moves the blue squares apart horizontally.
Wait, I just realized something. When I clicked on the left eye socket (row 16, col 21), the blue squares moved horizontally. But the left blue square was at cols 19-23, and the left eye socket is at cols 19-23 (approximately). So clicking on the left eye socket caused the blue squares to move apart horizontally.
Maybe the MOUSE action moves the blue squares horizontally based on where I click. If I click on the left side of the face, the blue squares move left. If I click on the right side of the face, the blue squares move right.
But clicking on the right eye socket didn't move the blue squares. Maybe the blue squares are already at their maximum horizontal positions.
Let me try clicking on a different region to see if the blue squares move. Maybe I need to click on the yellow or orange regions to move the blue squares.
Actually, let me step back and think about the goal. The face has two blue squares at the bottom. The blue squares can move up, down, and horizontally. The goal might be to move the blue squares to specific positions to form a "face" with eyes and a mouth.
Looking at the face, the blue squares are at the bottom of the black shape. The black shape has "eye socket" regions at rows 14-18. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions.
But the blue squares can only reach rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right) when they move up. They can't reach rows 14-18 using UP/DOWN actions.
Wait, maybe the goal is not to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to specific positions where they form a "smile" or "frown" on the face.
Looking at the face, the blue squares are at the bottom of the black shape, forming a "smile" or "frown". The left blue square is at cols 14-18, and the right blue square is at cols 44-48. The black shape extends from cols 9 to cols 53. So the blue squares are near the edges of the black shape.
Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the center of the black shape. The center of the black shape is approximately at cols 31. So the left blue square should be at cols 26-30, and the right blue square should be at cols 32-36.
But the blue squares can only move horizontally when I press MOUSE on certain regions. And clicking on the left eye socket caused the blue squares to move apart horizontally, not towards the center.
Let me try clicking on the right side of the face to see if the blue squares move towards the center.
Actually, I think I need to try a different approach. Let me look at the initial state of the game to see what the blue squares looked like at the start.
Looking at the history, the initial state had the blue squares at rows 54-58, cols 19-23 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 39-43 (right). So the blue squares were at the same row (rows 54-58), but at different columns.
The blue squares moved horizontally when I clicked on the left eye socket (row 16, col 21). The left blue square moved from cols 19-23 to cols 14-18 (5 columns left), and the right blue square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48 (5 columns right).
So clicking on the left eye socket caused the blue squares to move apart horizontally. Maybe clicking on the right side of the face causes the blue squares to move towards the center.
Let me try clicking on the right side of the face (row 30, col 45) to see if the blue squares move towards the center.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue square id={node['id']} bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")

# Let me try clicking on the right side of the face to see if the blue squares move towards the center
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 45}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Blue square id=5 bbox=(54,14)-(58,18)
Blue square id=6 bbox=(54,44)-(58,48)
